title: Prayer, Part 5: For Whom and What Should We Pray?
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

subpoints: A. Prayer is Trinitarian: to the Father, through the Son, by the Spirit
B. Today: the content of prayer — for whom and what should we pray

outline.point: II. WLC Q183: For Whom Are We to Pray?
subpoints: A. The whole Church of Christ on earth — because of spiritual unity under one head
B. Magistrates and ministers — because both state and church are divine institutions
C. Ourselves, our brethren, our enemies, and all sorts of men living or to live hereafter

outline.point: III. For Whom We Are Not to Pray
subpoints: A. Not for the dead — Hebrews 9:27 establishes that death fixes one's state
B. The doctrine of purgatory as the false basis for prayers for the dead
C. Not for those known to have sinned the sin unto death (1 John 5:16)

outline.point: IV. Identifying the Sin unto Death
subpoints: A. Likely corresponds to the blasphemy against the Holy Spirit (Matthew 12:31)
B. Not a sin too great to be forgiven but one with no desire to repent
C. Extreme caution in identifying any individual as having committed it — when in doubt, pray

outline.point: V. WLC Q184: What Should We Pray For?
subpoints: A. All things tending to the glory of God
B. The welfare of the church — as goes the church, so goes everything else
C. Our own and others' good, but not for anything unlawful — Scripture already answers those questions

summary:
In Part 5 of the prayer series, Pastor Tito Lyro addresses Westminster Larger Catechism questions 183 and 184, which together cover for whom and for what we should pray. He notes that Scripture's priorities in prayer are often the reverse of ours: there is a direct command to pray for civil leaders and for the church, but no direct command to pray for unsaved family members (though it is implied). The catechism lists the whole Church of Christ on earth as the first and primary focus of prayer, followed by magistrates and ministers, then ourselves, our brethren, and even our enemies.

Lyro also treats those for whom we are not to pray: the dead, whose state is fixed at death, and those known to have committed the sin unto death. He connects this unpardonable sin to the blasphemy of the Holy Spirit and to the warning of Hebrews 6 — a permanent, willful rejection of the Spirit's work with no remaining desire for repentance. On the question of what to pray for, he teaches that the primary consideration must always be the glory of God, which requires knowing the Bible. Prayer and Scripture study are inseparable: we cannot know what glorifies God without studying His Word.
transcript:
## Introduction: The Content of Prayer

You know, I'm so thankful that there are men and women who have gone before us, who are smarter than me, and are able to think through things and organize them in a very helpful way — and that's what we find with the Larger Catechism. So far, as we're talking about prayer, we saw what prayer is. Then we saw that prayer should only be offered to the God of the Bible. Then we saw that prayer can only be offered in Jesus' name. And last week we saw that the Holy Spirit helps us when we pray. When we put all this together, we see that Christian prayer is Trinitarian — involves the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit, each one with a function in it.

Our catechism takes us today to the content of our prayers — both the subjects of our prayers (for whom we pray) and the content (the things we pray for).

So let's start with a real question: for whom should we pray? Family. People who are still alive. The church. Our children. Everyone. God Himself. Magistrates. Our enemies.

Do you realize that there's no direct command to pray for unsaved family members? It's implied in different things, but there's no direct command. And yet when you hear our prayers, you'd think that was the first command given. Do you know that there's a direct command to pray for your political leaders? And yet when you hear our prayers, there's not a lot of that. There's a direct command to pray for missions. And yet when we hear our prayers, it doesn't seem to reflect that as much. So it's important that when we think of for whom we should pray, we put the same emphasis that the Scriptures put on that.

## WLC Q183: For Whom Are We to Pray?

Question 183 of the Larger Catechism asks, for whom are we to pray? The answer is: we are to pray for the whole Church of Christ upon earth, for magistrates and ministers, for ourselves, our brethren and enemies, and for all sorts of men living or that shall live hereafter — but not for the dead, nor for those that are known to have sinned the sin unto death.

### The Whole Church of Christ

The first thing we must pray for is the whole Church of Christ on earth, because of the spiritual unity of the Church as one body under Christ the head. Christ prayed for the whole church in John 17. In Ephesians 3, Paul prays for the church. The second petition of the Lord's Prayer — thy kingdom come — is a prayer for the whole Church of Christ.

That means we are not to limit our prayers to the branch of the Church of which we ourselves are members. We're praying for the Church in general. That also implies that we need to be informed about what's going on in the Church in general in order to be able to pray for it.

### Magistrates and Ministers

In 1 Timothy 2, Paul says, I exhort first of all that supplications, prayers, intercessions, and giving of thanks be made for all men — for kings and all who are in authority — that we may lead a quiet and peaceable life in all godliness and reverence. So if your life is not peaceful, one of the things you should ask yourself is: am I praying for those who have authority over me?

In Colossians 4:3-4, Paul says, praying also for us, that God would open to us a door for the word, to speak the mystery of Christ. In almost every letter in the New Testament there is a request by the author for the church to pray for the ministry of the Word of God. So both the state and the church are divine institutions for the accomplishment of God's purposes in the world, and both need wisdom, courage, honesty, and integrity.

If you think the civil government is not doing well, one of the ways to minister to them is by praying for them. We need to pray for the salvation of our political leaders. We need to pray for blessings upon their families that they will lead us in righteousness.

### Ourselves

Is there a direct command to pray for ourselves? Yes. Jesus himself prayed for his own well-being in John 17. We also pray for ourselves in the fourth, fifth, and sixth petitions of the Lord's Prayer: give us this day our daily bread; forgive us our debts; do not lead us into temptation. But we have to be careful that these prayers are not selfish. James 4:3: you ask and do not receive because you ask amiss, that you may spend it on your pleasures.

### Our Brethren and Enemies

We also ought to pray for our brethren — corporately and individually as Paul does throughout his letters. And we are to pray for our enemies. Matthew 5:44: I say to you, love your enemies, bless those who curse you, do good to those who hate you, and pray for those who spitefully use you and persecute you. Romans 12:14: bless those who persecute you. Bless and do not curse.

This can be a great thing regarding family relationships. Sometimes your spouse may seem as your enemy, and you might be tempted to be mean to them. And even if you see your spouse as your enemy, the command in the Bible is to pray for them and to love them.

### For the Living and Those Yet to Come

We are to pray for all people living and who will live. In 1 Timothy 2, Paul says supplications, prayers, intercessions, and giving of thanks be made for all men. Jesus himself said in John 17:20, I do not pray for these alone, but also for those who will believe in me through their word — and that's a prayer for us. He prayed for us 2,000 years ago, and he continues to pray for us before the throne of God.

## For Whom We Are Not to Pray

The catechism says we are not to pray for the dead. The reason is that the dead have their state fixed — either in heaven or in hell. Hebrews 9:27: it is appointed for men to die once, but after this, the judgment. Once you die, that's it. There's no changing there. This idea of praying for the dead arose from the doctrine of purgatory — the idea that souls can still be helped after death by the prayers and works of the living. But the Bible teaches that once you die, that's it.

And then the catechism says we are not to pray for those that are known to have sinned the sin unto death. That's a reference to 1 John 5:16, where John says: if anyone sees his brother sinning, a sin which does not lead to death, he will ask, and he will give him life for those who commit sin not leading to death. There is sin leading to death. I do not say that he should pray about that.

Theologians believe that the sin unto death may be the same as the blasphemy against the Holy Spirit found in the Synoptic Gospels. In Matthew 12:31, Jesus says, I say to you, every sin and blasphemy will be forgiven men, but the blasphemy against the Spirit will not be forgiven. This sin is unforgivable not because it is too great to be forgiven, but because the one who commits it has no desire to repent of it. Theologians understand this sin to be a stubborn, permanent, and complete resistance to the pleading of the Holy Spirit, which ultimately results in the Holy Spirit abandoning the person to his own sin.

We should, however, be extremely cautious about saying that a particular individual has sinned the sin unto death. If there is any reasonable doubt as to whether a person committed the sin unto death, we should pray for them.

## WLC Q184: What Should We Pray For?

Question 184 asks, for what things are we to pray? We are to pray for all things tending to the glory of God, the welfare of the church, our own or others' good, but not for any unlawful things.

The most important consideration in figuring out what to pray for is the glory of God. Is this going to bring glory to God? And the only way to know if something tends to God's glory is by studying the Scriptures. So prayer and Scripture study go together hand in hand.

We have to pray for the welfare of the church because it is the bride of Christ. As goes the church, so goes everything else. But we've put so little emphasis on corporate life as believers that we don't really care about the church. And yet life in the Bible is a corporate life. The unit in the Bible is not the individual — it's the family and the church.

We also pray for what is good for us. Matthew 7:7-11: ask, and it will be given to you. What man is there, if his son asks for bread, will give him a stone? How much more will your Father who is in heaven give good things to those who ask him?

And we will not pray for anything that goes against the Word of God. If the Bible forbids something, we don't need to pray about it. You don't need to pray whether you should date an unbeliever — the Bible already says no. You don't need to pray whether you should look at pornography — the Bible already says no. You need to know the Bible in order to pray appropriate prayers.

Let's pray. Glorious God, we thank you that we can come in prayer to you. We thank you that you answer our prayers because we come in the name of the Lord Jesus. We thank you for revealing yourself to us so that we can pray to the true God of the Bible. We thank you that your Spirit works in us so that we pray appropriately, and he brings our prayers before you. Father, pray that you would bless your church, that you would bless also the state. We pray that we would grow in the knowledge of Christ. We pray that those who have the rule over us would grow in righteousness, that they will lead us in righteousness. We pray these things in Jesus' name. Amen.
